canonicalTitleEn: Albert Einstein failed mathematics in school
canonicalTitleZh: 愛因斯坦數學不及格
notes: Einstein excelled at mathematics from a young age, mastering calculus by about 15; the myth likely arose from a misreading of a Swiss grading scale (where 6, not 1, was the top mark) and he himself dismissed the claim.
primarySource: Einstein biographies and Princeton archives (Walter Isaacson, 'Einstein')
provenance: A widely repeated misconception explicitly debunked by historians and by Einstein during his lifetime.

canonicalTitleEn: Columbus proved the Earth was round against believers in a flat Earth
canonicalTitleZh: 哥倫布證明地球是圓的
notes: Educated Europeans had known the Earth was spherical since antiquity (Eratosthenes even measured its circumference); the real dispute with Columbus was that he badly underestimated the Earth's size, and his critics were correct that Asia was too far to reach.
primarySource: Jeffrey Burton Russell, 'Inventing the Flat Earth'
provenance: A well-documented 19th-century myth, popularized partly by Washington Irving's fictionalized biography of Columbus.

canonicalTitleEn: Humans only use 10 percent of their brains
canonicalTitleZh: 人類只使用大腦的百分之十
notes: Brain imaging shows that virtually all of the brain is active over a day and even simple tasks engage many regions; no large dormant portion exists, and damage to almost any area causes deficits.
primarySource: neuroscience consensus (Barry Beyerstein's analyses; functional neuroimaging studies)
provenance: A popular self-help and pop-culture myth repeatedly identified as false by neuroscientists.

canonicalTitleEn: Lemmings deliberately commit mass suicide by jumping off cliffs
canonicalTitleZh: 旅鼠集體跳崖自殺
notes: Lemming populations fluctuate and they disperse en masse, sometimes drowning while crossing water, but they do not intentionally kill themselves; the belief was cemented by the 1958 Disney film 'White Wilderness', whose makers staged the scene by driving lemmings off a cliff.
primarySource: investigations into Disney's 'White Wilderness' (1958); zoological studies of Lemmus populations
provenance: A documented myth traced to a staged nature documentary.

canonicalTitleEn: The forbidden fruit in the Genesis Garden of Eden was an apple
canonicalTitleZh: 伊甸園的禁果是蘋果
notes: The Book of Genesis never names the fruit; the apple association in the Western tradition likely arose from a Latin pun between 'malum' (evil) and 'malum' (apple) in the Vulgate and later art.
primarySource: biblical text (Genesis 3); scholarship on the Latin Vulgate translation
provenance: A well-documented misreading of scripture noted by biblical scholars.

canonicalTitleEn: An apple fell on Isaac Newton's head, instantly revealing gravity
canonicalTitleZh: 蘋果砸中牛頓的頭
notes: Newton's own account (via William Stukeley and John Conduitt) describes merely seeing an apple fall in his orchard, which prompted him to reflect on gravity over years of work; there is no contemporary evidence an apple struck his head or that insight was instantaneous.
primarySource: William Stukeley, 'Memoirs of Sir Isaac Newton's Life' (1752)
provenance: A popular embellishment of a real anecdote, corrected by Newton scholars.

canonicalTitleEn: Galileo dropped balls from the Leaning Tower of Pisa to test gravity
canonicalTitleZh: 伽利略在比薩斜塔扔球
notes: The only source for this is a late biography by Galileo's pupil Vincenzo Viviani; Galileo himself never recorded it, and his work on falling bodies relied chiefly on inclined-plane experiments and thought experiments.
primarySource: Vincenzo Viviani's biography of Galileo (1654); history-of-science scholarship
provenance: A widely doubted anecdote regarded by historians of science as likely apocryphal.

canonicalTitleEn: The accused witches of Salem were burned at the stake
canonicalTitleZh: 塞勒姆女巫被火刑燒死
notes: In the 1692 Salem trials nineteen people were hanged and one (Giles Corey) was pressed to death; none were burned, as burning was not a legal penalty in colonial Massachusetts.
primarySource: court records of the Salem witch trials (1692)
provenance: A common misconception, possibly conflated with continental European burnings, corrected by primary trial records.

canonicalTitleEn: Marie Antoinette said 'Let them eat cake' about starving peasants
canonicalTitleZh: 瑪麗·安托瓦內特說讓他們吃蛋糕
notes: The phrase ('Qu'ils mangent de la brioche') appears in Rousseau's 'Confessions', written when Marie Antoinette was a child, attributed to an unnamed 'great princess'; there is no evidence she ever said it.
primarySource: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, 'Confessions' (Book VI, written c. 1765)
provenance: A famous misattribution documented by historians of the French Revolution.

canonicalTitleEn: Emperor Nero fiddled while Rome burned
canonicalTitleZh: 尼祿在羅馬大火時拉小提琴
notes: The fiddle (a bowed instrument) did not exist in the 1st century CE; ancient sources disagree on Nero's conduct during the 64 CE fire, and Tacitus reports he was at Antium and organized relief efforts, though rumors of him singing later circulated.
primarySource: Tacitus, 'Annals' (Book XV)
provenance: A widely recognized anachronism and embellishment corrected against ancient sources.

canonicalTitleEn: Roman gladiators always fought to the death
canonicalTitleZh: 羅馬角鬥士總是戰至死亡
notes: Gladiators were expensive investments who underwent long training, so most matches ended without death; estimates suggest only a minority of bouts were fatal, and defeated fighters were often spared.
primarySource: archaeological and epigraphic studies of gladiatorial combat (e.g. gladiator cemeteries and inscriptions)
provenance: A popular-culture exaggeration corrected by modern classical scholarship.

canonicalTitleEn: Romans used special rooms called vomitoria to vomit during feasts
canonicalTitleZh: 古羅馬人在嘔吐室嘔吐
notes: A vomitorium was an architectural passage through which crowds 'spewed out' of an amphitheater or stadium; it had nothing to do with vomiting after meals.
primarySource: Latin architectural usage; Macrobius and studies of Roman amphitheater design
provenance: A well-documented misunderstanding of a Latin term, corrected by classicists.

canonicalTitleEn: The Pilgrims first stepped ashore on Plymouth Rock in 1620
canonicalTitleZh: 清教徒踏上普利茅斯岩
notes: No contemporary Pilgrim account mentions a landing rock; the Mayflower party first landed at Provincetown, and the Plymouth Rock identification first appears in 1741, over a century later.
primarySource: William Bradford, 'Of Plymouth Plantation'; the 1741 Elder Faunce account
provenance: A patriotic legend lacking primary support, documented by American historians.

canonicalTitleEn: Walt Disney's body was cryogenically frozen after his death
canonicalTitleZh: 華特·迪士尼被冷凍保存
notes: Walt Disney died in 1966 and was cremated; his remains are interred at Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Glendale, California.
primarySource: Disney death certificate and Forest Lawn interment records; biographies (Neal Gabler, 'Walt Disney')
provenance: A persistent urban legend refuted by documented records of his cremation.

canonicalTitleEn: Bulls are enraged by the color red
canonicalTitleZh: 公牛被紅色激怒
notes: Cattle are red-green colorblind; in bullfighting it is the movement of the cape (the muleta), not its red color, that provokes the bull, and the red hue traditionally serves to mask bloodstains.
primarySource: studies of bovine dichromatic vision
provenance: A common misconception disproven by research on cattle color perception.

canonicalTitleEn: Bats are blind
canonicalTitleZh: 蝙蝠是瞎的
notes: All bat species can see, and many fruit bats have excellent vision; echolocating bats use sound to navigate in darkness but are not blind.
primarySource: zoological studies of Chiroptera vision
provenance: A widespread folk belief contradicted by biology and reflected in the inaccurate phrase 'blind as a bat'.

canonicalTitleEn: Goldfish have only a three-second memory
canonicalTitleZh: 金魚只有三秒記憶
notes: Experiments show goldfish can remember learned tasks, routes, and feeding cues for weeks or months, demonstrating long-term memory.
primarySource: behavioral experiments on goldfish memory and learning
provenance: A popular myth repeatedly disproven by animal-cognition studies.

canonicalTitleEn: Cracking your knuckles causes arthritis
canonicalTitleZh: 掰指關節會導致關節炎
notes: Studies, including Donald Unger's self-experiment of cracking one hand's knuckles for decades, find no association between knuckle cracking and arthritis.
primarySource: Donald Unger's longitudinal self-study (Ig Nobel Prize, 2009) and clinical reviews
provenance: A common health myth not supported by medical evidence.

canonicalTitleEn: Different sections of the tongue detect different basic tastes
canonicalTitleZh: 舌頭不同區域感受不同味道
notes: All taste qualities can be sensed across the whole tongue wherever there are taste buds; the 'tongue map' arose from a mistranslation and misinterpretation of a 1901 German study by D.P. Hanig.
primarySource: Virginia Collings (1974) and analyses of the misread Hanig (1901) data
provenance: A textbook myth corrected by sensory-science research.

canonicalTitleEn: Hair and fingernails continue to grow after a person dies
canonicalTitleZh: 人死後頭髮指甲繼續生長
notes: Growth requires living cellular processes that cease at death; the appearance is an illusion caused by the skin dehydrating and retracting, making hair and nails look longer.
primarySource: forensic and dermatological consensus
provenance: A common misconception explained by post-mortem skin desiccation.

canonicalTitleEn: The Coriolis effect determines which way water spins down toilets and drains
canonicalTitleZh: 科氏力決定馬桶排水的旋轉方向
notes: At the scale of a sink or toilet the Coriolis force is negligible; drain rotation is governed by basin shape, water jets, and residual motion, not by the hemisphere.
primarySource: fluid-dynamics analysis of the Coriolis effect at small scales
provenance: A widespread misconception corrected by physicists.

canonicalTitleEn: A penny dropped from a skyscraper can kill a person on the ground
canonicalTitleZh: 從摩天大樓扔下的硬幣能致命
notes: A penny's low mass and tumbling flight give it a terminal velocity of roughly 25 mph (about 40 km/h), enough to sting but not to penetrate the skull or cause fatal injury.
primarySource: physics of terminal velocity (tested on MythBusters and by physicists)
provenance: A popular myth refuted by aerodynamics and physical testing.

canonicalTitleEn: The Great Wall of China is the only human-made structure visible from the Moon
canonicalTitleZh: 長城是月球上唯一可見的人造建築
notes: From the Moon no individual human structure is visible to the naked eye; the Great Wall is narrow and blends with terrain, and astronauts have confirmed they could not see it from lunar distance.
primarySource: astronaut accounts (e.g. Neil Armstrong, Eugene Cernan) and NASA statements
provenance: A widely circulated claim contradicted by astronauts who have been to the Moon.

canonicalTitleEn: The Immaculate Conception refers to the virginal conception of Jesus
canonicalTitleZh: 聖母無原罪指耶穌的受孕
notes: In Catholic doctrine the Immaculate Conception is the belief that Mary was conceived without original sin; the conception of Jesus by the Virgin Mary is a separate doctrine (the Virgin Birth or Incarnation).
primarySource: Catholic dogma defined by Pope Pius IX, 'Ineffabilis Deus' (1854)
provenance: A common conflation of two distinct doctrines, clarified by Catholic theology.

canonicalTitleEn: Mrs. O'Leary's cow started the Great Chicago Fire by kicking over a lantern
canonicalTitleZh: 歐萊里太太的牛引發芝加哥大火
notes: The fire of 1871 did begin near the O'Leary barn, but the cow-and-lantern story was invented by a reporter who later admitted fabricating it, and the Chicago City Council formally exonerated Catherine O'Leary in 1997.
primarySource: reporter Michael Ahern's admission; 1997 Chicago City Council exoneration
provenance: A scapegoating legend documented as a fabricated newspaper story.

canonicalTitleEn: Napoleon's troops shot off the nose of the Great Sphinx of Giza
canonicalTitleZh: 拿破崙軍隊轟掉了獅身人面像的鼻子
notes: Sketches by the Danish traveler Frederic Louis Norden, drawn in 1737 and published before Napoleon's 1798 Egyptian campaign, already show the Sphinx without its nose, disproving the story.
primarySource: Frederic Louis Norden's drawings (1737, published 1755)
provenance: A popular myth refuted by pre-Napoleonic illustrations of the Sphinx.

canonicalTitleEn: Thomas Edison invented the first light bulb
canonicalTitleZh: 愛迪生發明了第一個燈泡
notes: Incandescent lamps were demonstrated by inventors such as Humphry Davy, Warren de la Rue, and Joseph Swan before Edison; Edison's 1879 contribution was a practical, longer-lasting, commercially viable bulb and lighting system, not the original concept.
primarySource: history of incandescent lighting (Joseph Swan's patents and Edison's improvements)
provenance: A simplification of invention history corrected by historians of technology.

canonicalTitleEn: George Washington had wooden teeth
canonicalTitleZh: 華盛頓有一副木製假牙
notes: Washington's dentures were made from materials such as human and animal teeth, ivory, and metal; none were wooden, and the 'wooden' appearance may reflect staining of the ivory over time.
primarySource: study of Washington's surviving dentures (Mount Vernon and Smithsonian collections)
provenance: A persistent myth contradicted by examination of his actual dentures.
